query: Foramen magnum contents and vertebral artery pathway

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Foramen Magnum -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The vertebral arteries and the anterior and posterior spinal arteries traverse the foramen magnum. The vertebral artery branches off of the subclavian artery and has four segments. The first preforaminal segment (V1) constitutes the portion from the subclavian to the transverse foramen of C6. The second foraminal segment (V2) travels through the transverse foramen of C6 through C2. When the artery emerges from the foramen of C2, it is considered V3. This third segment, the extradural segment, continues through the transverse foramen of C1, the suboccipital triangle, and the foramen magnum. The point at which the artery penetrates the dura and arachnoid mater indicates the beginning of the fourth and final segment of the vertebral artery, the intradural segment. The right and left fourth segments of the vertebral artery combine to form the basilar artery at the level of the pons. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Anterior Spinal Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The brain and the spinal cord receive their blood supply from the carotid arteries which branch from the aortic arch and the vertebral arteries. These branch off from the central subclavian arteries. The internal carotid artery is the main supplier of nutrient-rich blood to the brain. The spinal cord, however, receives its supply from the branches of the vertebral arteries. Each artery courses along the side of the neck forming a single basilar artery in the brain superiorly while branching bilaterally inferiorly to form a single anterior spinal artery at the level of the foramen magnum. This supplies the anterior portion of the spinal cord which covers most except for the dorsal columns. These are more posterior and require tributaries to supply this part. The dorsal columns receive blood supply by the right and left posterior spinal arteries which also originate from the vertebral artery and together, these travel in the subarachnoid space.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Blood Supply -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Vertebrobasilar System
retrieved_docs.content: The intradural portion of the vertebral artery (VA) courses anteromedially through the foramen magnum, runs superomedially toward the midline where the two VAs unite to form the basilar artery (BA). [9] During its journey to BA, VA gives the posteroinferior cerebellar Artery (PICA), the anterior spinal artery which supplies the upper spinal cord, lateral medulla, tonsils, the vermis, and the inferior cerebellar hemisphere. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Foramen Magnum -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to the basilar artery, the fourth segment of the vertebral arteries gives rise to the anterior spinal artery, the posterior spinal artery, perforating branches to the medulla, and the posterior inferior cerebellar artery. The anterior spinal artery feeds the upper cervical spinal cord and inferior medulla while the posterior spinal artery feeds the dorsal spinal cord and conus medullaris. The lateral medulla, cerebellar tonsils, inferior vermis, and choroid plexus depend on the posterior inferior cerebellar arteries, and the penetrating branches supply portions of the medulla, the olives, and the inferior cerebellar peduncle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Vertebral Canal Blood Supply -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The vertebral blood supply for the spinal cord comes from many different sources in the body depending on the region. The main blood supply to the spinal cord is via the single anterior spinal artery (ASA) and the two posterior spinal arteries (PSA). The anterior spinal artery is formed by the vertebral arteries which originate from the first part of the subclavian artery. The vertebral arteries pass through the transverse foramen of C1 through C6 and through the foramen magnum to become the basilar artery. However, before joining together to become the basilar artery, the vertebral arteries each give off a branch which becomes the anterior spinal artery; the anterior spinal artery travels caudally down the spinal cord through the anterior sulcus. [4] The posterior spinal arteries can branch off the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or branches from the pre-atlantal vertebral arteries which also travel caudally down the spinal cord but through the two posterior sulci. [5] The ASA provides blood to the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, and the PSA delivers blood to the posterior one-third of the spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Cervical Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial supply to the cervical spinal cord displays a marked variation between individuals because of differences in embryonic development—the ASA and two PSAs branch off the distal vertebral arteries at the level of the foramen magnum. The ASA courses midline on the ventral sulcus of the spinal cord, while the PSAs travel parallel to each other in a paramedian location on the dorsal aspect. In the cervical spine, two or three supportive anterior and posterior radiculomedullary arteries branch from the vertebral arteries. The largest of these radiculomedullary vessels is the artery of cervical enlargement, usually identified between the C4-C8 vertebral levels, most often coursing along the C6 nerve root. The radiculomedullary arteries join with the ASA and PSAs to carry supplemental blood flow to these nervous tissues. The provision of supplemental flow to the cord is from branches of the vertebral, ascending cervical, and deep cervical arteries, and potentially the ascending pharyngeal and occipital arteries. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Vertebrobasilar System -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The vertebrobasilar system is comprised of bilateral vertebral arteries (VA) and an unpaired basilar artery (BA). The vertebral artery divides into four segments along its course. [1] The pre-foraminal or V1 segment arises as to the first branch of the subclavian artery, superior to the first rib, [2] and courses posteriorly between the anterior scalene and longus colli muscles. The VA then enters the transverse foramen (also known as the foramina transversarium , TF) of the sixth cervical vertebrae (C6), [3] alongside the descending vertebral venous plexus and accompanying sympathetic plexus as the foraminal or V2 segment. As the VA ascends through the cervical vertebrae, VA branches at each cervical level supply the surrounding musculature via the anterior spinal arteries. After exiting the TF of the axis, the atlanto or V3 segment of the VA courses laterally to traverse the TF of C1. The VA then travels over the posterior arch of the atlas and through the suboccipital triangle to enter the intracranial space through the foramen magnum as the intracranial, intradural, or V4 segment. Upon entering the cranium, the VA branches off the posterior inferior cerebellar artery before it unites with the contralateral VA at the base of the pons to form the BA.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Vertebral Canal Blood Supply -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The anterior spinal artery and PSA are fed additional arteries throughout their course down the spinal cord at each spinal level through the intervertebral foramen. These additional arteries are called segmental spinal arteries. The segmental arteries then bifurcate into an anterior and posterior radicular artery which feed into both anterior and posterior spinal arteries respectively. Not only can they supply the ASA/PSA but also provide blood supply to the nerve roots along which they track. Theoretically there should be 31 radicular arteries for each foramen that receives an artery, but in reality, there is always less. [2] Sometimes, however, the spinal segmental arteries also give off segmental medullary arteries which directly feed into the anterior spinal artery and posterior spinal arteries, however, the numbers differ between person to person. There are thought to be anywhere from 8 to 10 in every person. The two anterior spinal arteries primarily supply the upper cervical spinal cord as they come off the vertebral arteries before they anastomose together into the single ASA. [4] The mid cervical spinal cord receives vascular supply by segmental medullary arteries coming from directly off the vertebral artery. The lower cervical to the upper thoracic spinal cord is supplied by radiculomedullary arteries arising from the cervicothoracic trunk C7 to T1 (either left or right). [2] Segmental spinal arteries supply the mid-thoracic region (T3 to T9) as they come off usually the posterior intercostal artery. The largest segmental medullary artery is the artery of Adamkiewicz also known as the greater anterior radiculomedullary artery. The artery of the Adamkiewicz (AKA) branches off the left side of the descending aorta around T8 to L2 (via lumbar or intercostal arteries) and connects on the ventral side of the spinal cord to supply the anterior spinal artery. [5] [6] Of note, the artery of Adamkiewicz delivers vascular supply a large area of the thoracolumbar region and thus is considered a watershed area. Branches from the internal iliac arteries supply the lumbosacral spinal cord. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Foramen Magnum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The foramen magnum is the largest foramen of the skull. It is located in the most inferior portion of the cranial fossa as a part of the occipital bone. Its contents include the medulla oblongata, meninges, spinal root of cranial nerve XI, vertebral arteries, anterior and posterior spinal arteries, the tectorial membrane, and alar ligaments. On the foramen magnum, there are two craniometric points: the basion, the median point of the front edge of the hole, and the opisthion, posterior correspondence.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Skull Foramen -- Structure and Function -- Posterior fossa
retrieved_docs.content: Foramen magnum-the largest of the cranial foramen and contains the following structures: Medulla Paired vertebral arteries Paired anterior spinal arteries Paired posterior spinal arteries Ascending portion of CN XI Dural veins

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Foramen Magnum -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomical variance of the foramen magnum may have some impact on certain surgical procedures such as vertebral artery and posterior inferior cerebellar artery aneurysm repairs, foramen magnum meningioma resections, and foramen magnum decompression among others. In an ovoid type foramen magnum, it is difficult for a surgeon to adequately expose the anterior portion of the foramen. Additionally, the occipital condyle and jugular tubercle are the main bony prominence obstructing the anterolateral portion of the foramen magnum. Extension of the occipital condyles into the foramen magnum may indicate the need for more extensive bony removal in certain procedures. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Reticular Formation -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: There is a profuse blood supply to the reticular formation due to the diffuse and expansive location of the network of brainstem nuclei and neurons. Blood supply to the brainstem, and thus, to the reticular formation, originates from the vertebrobasilar system or posterior circulation of the brain. The beginning of the posterior brain circulation system arises from the vertebral arteries, branches of the subclavian arteries. The vertebral arteries take a superior course towards the brain through the transverse foramen of the cervical vertebra. As the vertebral arteries then travel into the dural space, in an anteromedial direction through the foramen magnum of the skull, they unite to form a single basilar artery. Before the union of the vertebral arteries, the posterior inferior cerebellar artery, as well as the anterior spinal artery, arise, supplying blood flow to the inferior cerebellar hemispheres bilaterally, the cerebellar vermis and tonsils, the lateral medulla oblongata, and the upper cervical spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Occipital Bone, Artery, Vein, and Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The basilar part makes up the anterior portion of the foramen magnum on the undersurface of the occipital bone and connects to the sphenoid bone. It is thick and quadrilateral in shape. The anterior atlantooccipital membrane attaches to its inferior surface immediately anterior to the foramen magnum. Even more anterior to the foramen magnum is the pharyngeal tubercle, the attachment site of the fibrous raphe of the pharynx. On either side of the midline of its inferior surface, the basilar part provides muscular attachment of the longus capitis and rectus capitis anterior muscles. The upper surface of the basilar part supports the medulla oblongata with its broad, shallow groove called the clivus, where the abducens nerve also runs nearby. It also provides attachment to the tectorial membrane near the foramen magnum. The lateral surface of the basilar part forms a junction with the petrous portion of the temporal bone to form the inferior petrosal sulcus, in which the inferior petrosal sinus lies. The inferior petrosal sinus receives veins from the medulla, pons, the inferior surface of the cerebellum, and the internal auditory veins and drains into the internal jugular vein. The inner basilar part contains the basion, as previously mentioned.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Anterior Spinal Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The anterior spinal artery is the major blood supply for the spinal cord. It descends from the level of the foramen magnum along the anterior surface of the spinal cord. As it continues to descend, the artery receives communication with other branches that enter the spinal canal through the intervertebral foramen. The artery of Adamkiewicz anastomosis with the anterior spinal artery which feeds the lower thoracic, lumbar, and sacral spinal cord. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Spinal Artery Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying pathophysiology of anterior spinal artery syndrome is better understood by having familiarity with the anatomy. The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the medulla and spinal cord and is formed at the level of the foramen magnum by 2 arteries branching from their respective vertebral arteries. This artery is uninterrupted within the anterior median sulcus up to the conus medullaris. Although the anterior spinal artery is a continuous artery, multiple anastomotic networks are supplied by the anterior medullar or radiculomedullary arteries. The anterior spinal artery varies in caliber along the course, with the smallest diameter at the thoracic level, making this segment most vulnerable to ischemia. The largest diameter of this artery is at the lumbar level.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Artery of Adamkiewicz -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord receives its vascular supply from 3 main arteries. The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior 2/3, while the 2 posterolateral spinal arteries supply the posterior 3rd. [1] The anterior spinal artery originates from the 2 vertebral arteries at the level of the foramen magnum. Anterior segmental medullary vessels from the aorta provide additional supply, with the largest being the artery of Adamkiewicz, also known as the arteria radicularis magna or the great anterior radiculomedullary artery (see Image . Artery of Adamkiewicz).

Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration includes cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) or supratentorial regions,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

Neurosurgical exploration includes both craniectomy and craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, with infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial variants identified as specific methods. Suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploration or decompression of cranial nerves represents another dedicated technique, while transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal extends the scope of exploratory surgery to the orbit. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that provide access to and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without accompanying facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are detailed for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic regions, and for more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine, each described as a method that may be included in an overall exploration procedure. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

Excision serves as a parent node for “Clinical actions A‑G” and is linked to disease‑specific diagnostic pathways, exemplified by its role as the primary diagnostic procedure for colorectal cancer. The only procedural exclusion from the excision category is a thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that spans more than two vertebral segments and does not incorporate facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. retrieved_docs.content: Excision functions as an overarching surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques. Specific procedures classified as methods of Excision are Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally as a separate procedure); Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct accompanied by cholangiography; Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography); Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; Exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with decompression of cervical nerve roots, including partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and excision of herniated intervertebral disc; Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; Parathyroidectomy or exploration

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly examine an anatomic region to diagnose, treat, or remove pathology. The category includes adrenalectomy—partial or complete removal of the adrenal gland performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy—and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, is classified as an exploration method, as is choledochotomy or choledochostomy performed for ductal exploration, drainage, or stone removal, sometimes accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by craniectomy or craniotomy performed for supratentorial investigation and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration procedures consist of laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these are specified for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and thoracic regions, and for more than two thoracic segments, with the latter variations noted as possibly included methods. Urologic and endocrine exploration includes radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration. The broader classification “Clinical actions A‑G” serves as a parent category for exploration procedures, indicating that these diverse surgical methods are organized under a common clinical action framework. Together, these procedures illustrate how direct anatomical exploration is integral to the diagnosis, management, and definitive treatment of diseases across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures comprise a diverse group of surgical interventions that are performed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in multiple anatomic regions. In the endocrine system, both partial and complete adrenalectomy, as well as adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy, can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may include removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy combined with common bile duct exploration, which may be performed with cholangiography, and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate ductal exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and optional transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region. Orthopedic exploration focuses on laminectomy with spinal cord or cauda equina exploration and decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these techniques are applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic regions, and may be extended to more than two segments in the cervical and lumbar spine. Minimally invasive exploration includes laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration. Urologic exploration is exemplified by radical orchiectomy for tumor removal accompanied by abdominal exploration. Finally, parathyroid exploration procedures involve parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed either through a cervical approach or a transthoracic approach. Some of these procedures are noted as having possibly included methods, indicating that variations or additional steps may be incorporated depending on the clinical scenario. Together, these exploration procedures reflect a comprehensive surgical strategy to diagnose, access, and manage disease across multiple organ systems.

Within the procedural taxonomy, Excision serves as a parent category for “Clinical actions A – G,” indicating that these clinical actions are sub‑categories of the broader excision concept. At the same time, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision grouping; these include laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, thoracic, or sacral regions, as well as multisegment laminectomy (> 2 vertebral segments) in the cervical and thoracic regions. This delineation clarifies that such laminectomy techniques are not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a wide range of surgical techniques used to investigate, diagnose, or treat pathology in diverse anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland operations such as the partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal exploration performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, which may also involve biopsy and, when indicated, excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Biliary exploration is represented by cholecystectomy with common duct investigation, either through cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, and can be performed laparoscopically. Cranial exploration procedures comprise exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial compartment and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at decompression of cranial nerves, as well as transcranial orbital exploration for lesion removal. Spinal exploration is addressed by multilevel laminectomies of the cervical, thoracic, or lumbar spine that allow decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, and by lumbar laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc fragment excision in a single‑level re‑exploration. Parathyroid exploration procedures include parathyroidectomy with mediastinal access achieved through cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic approaches. All of these specific methods are organized under the broader parent category “Clinical actions A‑G,” which groups related exploratory surgical interventions for systematic clinical documentation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.
